The Pipe Organ Rocks – Modal Toccata
#music

  *   * Share on X (Opens in new window) X

When someone says “pipe organ”, most people probably think of someone playing hymns on it. Well, these players play a lot more than hymns.

## Organic Pipes

I’ve been playing pipe organ since I was in graduate school. To my good fortune, Mr. Emmett G. Smith was solely at TCU to teach this instrument. So, he was my teacher at that time. In those days, I did learn a lot of hymns and other “church” music.

But I also learned a lot about “registrations” (which stops are pulled to shape how the pipe organ sounds) and pedal work. Oh yes: learning to play a full set of pedals at the same time as two or more manuals (keyboards) was fun and frustrating.

Rehearsing my first toccata on the pipe organ is quite memorable. In my case, it was the last movement of Suite Gothique, Opus 25, by Léon Boëllmann. At first, I thought that I’d never get that repeating phrase solid. Fortunately, over time, I did.

MODAL TOCCATA was written for the pipe organ … but you might be surprised how it sounds.

Click To Tweet

## This Pipe Rocks, Too

Still, I was influenced by a host of different styles of music. I grew up a preacher’s kid. Obviously, this meant spending time in the church, but I was also in the youth culture of the 1970s and 1980s. Plus, I was in bands of several diverse influences.

One of those bands was a jazz-rock-fusion quartet. The players were exceptional, especially for teenagers. Besides learning tunes, we would improvise for hours.

Typically, one of us would create a “theme”. From there, other players would embellish or create counterpoint. Eventually, we’d wander off into other musical tangents during this same improvisation. However, the goal was always to come back to that initial theme. Often, the player who originated the theme would not be the one who brought it back. Another favorite improv technique was that we’d all play the theme in unison to complete the jam.

Jazz-Rock-Fusion pipe organ music? Maybe. #Listen

Click To Tweet

## Improv Organ

Because of the repetition, those themes would often stay in my memory for days, weeks, or months. A few of them stayed with me for years.

I know this because several years after leaving that band, I played some of them again. As I was improvising during a church service on the pipe organ, I played one of those fusion improv themes. It sort of snuck back into my brain. Later that same day, I began to compose “Modal Toccata” using the influences of some of those previously improvised themes.

Finally, to bring this piece full circle, as I recorded the toccata, I improvised a brand new cadenza. It was not the first time I had spontaneously created this third section. However, for now, this one is my favorite version.

Organ improvisation is not just for blues players.

Click To Tweet

## Tech and More

Now, you get to hear the influences of church, jazz-rock, improv, and who knows what else in one piece. Listen to “Modal Toccata” in all its glory on a grand set of pipes.

Well honestly, the instrument in this recording is not one pipe organ. Instead, I’ve layered several. In other words, it would take a lot of work and technology to hear this instrument “live”. That’s because it would require that we synchronize the sound from at least five different cathedrals and theatres.

For those of you interested in adding “Modal Toccata” to your repertoire, I am creating sheet music of this piece. It will be available very soon. Write to me if you’d like to be notified when you can pick up your copy.


    The featured photo (top of the page) is by Eric Mak and the Modal Toccata album cover art is by Michael Jasmund. Both were obtained through royalty-free services.

images.alt:
A fictional natural history illustration depicts Voutyrómyga Rex, an enormous fly-like predator standing in a muddy prehistoric swamp beneath dark, storm-filled skies. The creature dominates the frame from a low, ground-level perspective, making it appear colossal and intimidating. Its body is covered in coarse black bristles, and its oversized compound eyes glow with a deep reddish-brown color. Transparent insect wings stretch outward from either side, their delicate vein structures contrasting sharply with the monster’s brutal appearance. Long, spiked legs rise from the muddy ground like skeletal pillars, each segment covered with sharp protrusions and dark hairs. At the center of its face, multiple hooked mouthparts curve downward, dripping with moisture and suggesting a powerful predatory bite. The swamp around it is littered with shallow pools, rocks, and scattered debris, while distant palms and ancient vegetation fade into mist. A volcanic shape appears faintly in the background beneath the heavy clouds. Informational text frames the image like a museum display, presenting the creature as an extinct apex predator from the Late Cretaceous. The overall mood combines scientific illustration, creature design, and cinematic monster horror.
Keywords: giant fly, speculative evolution, prehistoric swamp, apex predator, insect monster, paleo fantasy, compound eyes, creature design, ancient ecosystem, cinematic horror
AI-generated; use is permitted for non-commercial creative projects. MidJourney, FLUX.1-schnell model via Perchance, Artspace.ai, REVE, Echoform™.

images.alt:
A colossal white dragon dominates the entire frame in an intense close-up portrait. The creature lunges directly toward the viewer through swirling snow and shattered ice, creating a powerful sense of motion and imminent danger. Its broad skull is covered in rough, weathered scales that resemble frozen stone, with sharp horns and jagged spines radiating backward from its crown. Deep scars and dark streaks of blood mark portions of its face, suggesting countless battles survived.
The dragon's mouth gapes open in a deafening roar, revealing multiple rows of long, uneven ivory fangs that curve inward like spears. A dark tongue twists upward from the depths of its throat, where a faint reddish glow burns through the shadows. Frost, mist, and debris fill the air around the beast, partially obscuring its massive body and emphasizing its overwhelming scale. One gleaming eye catches the light, fixed on its target with predatory intensity.
The palette is dominated by icy whites, pale grays, and cold blue tones, contrasted by subtle crimson accents around the mouth and wounds. Soft winter light filters through the haze, giving the scene a cinematic, high-fantasy atmosphere. The composition is centered and confrontational, placing the viewer directly in the path of the charging dragon.
Keywords: dragon, white dragon, frost, monster, fantasy creature, roar, fangs, ice, winter, close-up, scales, epic fantasy
AI-generated; use is permitted for non-commercial creative projects. MidJourney, FLUX.1-schnell model via Perchance, Artspace.ai, REVE, Echoform™.

images.alt:
A black-and-white photograph captures a solitary sunflower resting in a narrow glass bottle placed on a worn windowsill. The flower's stem curves gently upward before bending beneath the weight of the bloom, creating a graceful arch. Its petals are slightly wilted and irregular, some curling inward while others stretch outward in delicate, frayed shapes. The textured center of the sunflower remains visible despite the fading bloom, adding contrast against the soft petals. The bottle contains a small amount of water and shows age through streaks, imperfections, and cloudy reflections in the glass. The windowsill beneath is rough and weathered, marked by peeling paint and uneven textures that suggest years of use. Beyond the window, an urban streetscape dissolves into a soft blur, with indistinct building facades and faint shapes of distant pedestrians. Gentle daylight streams through the glass, illuminating the flower and creating subtle tonal transitions from bright highlights to deep shadows. The overall mood is quiet, reflective, and timeless, emphasizing beauty found in impermanence and decay.
Keywords:
sunflower, monochrome, still life, wilted flower, glass bottle, window light, urban backdrop, soft focus, texture, reflection, solitude, photography
AI-generated; use is permitted for non-commercial creative projects. MidJourney, FLUX.1-schnell model via Perchance, Artspace.ai, REVE, Echoform™.
